mnemonics.title: Dilation vs Erosion
mnemonics.content: DADD: Dilation Adds pixels (grows), Erosion Eliminates pixels (shrinks). Think: D for Dilate = Deepen (more white), E for Erode = Eat away (less white)

mnemonics.title: Opening vs Closing
mnemonics.content: OPEN the door to let things IN (erosion first, then dilation - removes small objects), CLOSE the door to keep things OUT (dilation first, then erosion - fills holes). Order matters!

mnemonics.title: Hit-or-Miss Requirements
mnemonics.content: HIT foreground, MISS background - both MUST be satisfied simultaneously for pattern detection

mnemonics.title: Structuring Element Origin
mnemonics.content: ORIGIN: Original Reference In Geometric Operations - the center point for positioning

acronyms.usage: A small binary kernel used in morphological operations to probe image structure
Pattern detection requiring match to both foreground and background patterns
Erosion followed by dilation: removes small objects and smooths boundaries
Dilation followed by erosion: fills small holes and connects nearby objects

visualTips.visualization: Imagine placing an eraser (SE) at each foreground pixel - if any part extends beyond the object, that pixel becomes background. Objects shrink by SE radius from all edges.
Imagine painting with a brush (SE) centered on each foreground pixel. The brush adds pixels wherever it touches background, causing objects to grow by SE radius.
Picture erosion as a filter that removes anything smaller than the SE, then dilation restores the general shape of remaining larger objects - like sieving stones and returning the survivors.
Dilation fills small holes first, then erosion shrinks back - like inflating balloons into cavities then deflating, leaving the cavities filled but not changing overall size much.

analogies.analogy: Like a doctor's stethoscope probes the body, a structuring element probes the image to detect specific geometric patterns.
Like finding a specific puzzle piece by checking both its shape (foreground) and the surrounding space (background) must match.
Just as erosion in geology wears away rock edges, morphological erosion wears away object boundaries.
Like matching fingerprints against a database of known prints, morphological prototype matching compares image patterns against known templates to identify classes.
